confidence.disclosure: Ibn Sina dictated the early part of his life story to his student Juzjani, who completed it after Avicenna's death. Between them they give us the cities of his life in order: Afshana, Bukhara, Gurganj, then a string of Khurasanian towns (Fasa, Baward, Tus, Samanqan, Jajarm), Jurjan, Rayy, Hamadan, Isfahan, and his death back at Hamadan in 1037. Years are reliable; months and exact transitions are not, because Juzjani himself was not present for the earlier parts and Ibn Sina was on the move during periods of political collapse. Some scholars place his move from Bukhara to Gurganj as early as 999 (the fall of the Samanids) and others as late as 1002 (his father's death) — both are recorded here as a year-precision range.

sources: Dimitri Gutas, Avicenna and the Aristotelian Tradition (1988, 2014)
Peter Adamson, Philosophy in the Islamic World (2016)

journey.event: Born in the village of Afshana near Bukhara, in the Samanid Empire. His father Abdullah, originally from Balkh, is the local governor; his mother Sitara is a Tajik woman from the same village.

Family moves to Bukhara, the Samanid capital and one of the great intellectual centres of the Islamic world. By age ten he has memorised the entire Qur'an and is being taught logic and philosophy by tutors, including the wandering scholar al-Natili.

Cures the Samanid sultan Nuh ibn Mansur of an illness that had baffled the court physicians. As a reward he is granted access to the royal library — a vast collection of rare manuscripts that he later credits as the foundation of his self-education.
His father dies. Bukhara has fallen to the Karakhanid Turks; the Samanid order in which his family had served is collapsing.

Leaves Bukhara out of necessity and travels north to Gurganj, capital of Khwarazm, where the Ma'munid ruler Ali ibn Ma'mun maintains a brilliant scholarly court. He may meet al-Biruni here for the first time, beginning a philosophical correspondence that lasts years.

Leaves Gurganj 'through necessity' as Mahmud of Ghazna's pressure on Khwarazm grows. Begins a period of wandering across Khurasan in search of a patron, passing through Fasa, Baward, Tus, Samanqan and Jajarm.

Arrives at Jurjan, near the Caspian Sea, hoping for the patronage of the Ziyarid ruler Qabus ibn Wushmagir — but Qabus has just been overthrown and killed. There he meets the disciple and scribe al-Juzjani, who will stay with him for the rest of his life and preserve his autobiography.
Begins writing Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb (the Canon of Medicine) in Jurjan. Lectures to a small circle on logic and astronomy.
Moves to Rayy, near present-day Tehran, and enters the service of the Buyid queen-regent al-Sayyida and her son Majd al-Dawla. Civil strife forces him to flee onward to Qazwin.

Settles in Hamadan in west-central Iran, summoned to treat the Buyid amir Shams al-Dawla for colic. He cures him and is appointed his vizier — by day administering the state, by night gathering students for philosophical discussion.
His students, led by Juzjani, ask him to write a comprehensive philosophical encyclopaedia. He begins Kitab al-Shifa (The Book of Healing), reportedly composing fifty pages a day.

Shams al-Dawla dies. Ibn Sina refuses to serve his successor and goes into hiding in the house of his friend Abu Ghalib al-Attar. From hiding he secretly contacts Ala al-Dawla, the Kakuyid ruler of Isfahan, offering his services.

His correspondence with Isfahan is discovered. Attar's house is ransacked and Ibn Sina is imprisoned in the fortress of Fardajan, outside Hamadan. He spends four months there, writing prolifically in his cell.
Released from Fardajan when Ala al-Dawla briefly captures Hamadan. Ibn Sina decides to escape to Isfahan, leaving Hamadan disguised as a Sufi ascetic, with his brother, Juzjani, and two slaves.

Arrives in Isfahan and is warmly received by the Kakuyid ruler Ala al-Dawla. The most stable period of his life begins: he serves as advisor and physician, accompanies the ruler on campaigns, and completes the Shifa and the Canon. For Ala al-Dawla he writes the Persian Danish-nama.
Completes Kitab al-Shifa, the vast philosophical and scientific encyclopaedia begun in Hamadan. Continues astronomical observations from Isfahan.
Ghaznavid forces briefly occupy Isfahan. Ibn Sina and Ala al-Dawla flee south into Khuzistan, where they remain until the death of Mahmud of Ghazna two months later.
Back in Isfahan, begins writing Al-Isharat wa'l-Tanbihat (Pointers and Reminders), the mature, condensed expression of his philosophical thought.

While accompanying Ala al-Dawla on campaign, Ibn Sina is struck by a severe colic — an ailment that had troubled him throughout his life. He treats himself, but the medicine is bungled by an attendant. He dies in Hamadan in the holy month of Ramadan, aged 57. He is buried where he died, and a mausoleum still stands over his grave.
